---
title: "PlasticIceSheet.jl"
subtitle: "Fast, AD-friendly perfectly-plastic ice-sheet reconstruction"
---

`PlasticIceSheet.jl` reconstructs a perfectly-plastic, steady-state ice sheet from three
inputs — a grounded-ice **margin/mask**, a **bed elevation** $z_b$, and a **basal shear
stress** field $\tau_b$ — and returns a glaciologically plausible ice **surface** $z_s$ and
**thickness** $H$. There is no time-stepping, no climate, and no ice dynamics.

It is a clean-room simplification and modernization of **ICESHEET 2.0** by Evan Gowan and
colleagues — [source code](https://github.com/evangowan/icesheet), the method's
description paper
([Gowan et al., 2016, *Geosci. Model Dev.*](https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-9-1673-2016)),
and its global application to the past 80,000 years
([Gowan et al., 2021, *Nat. Commun.*](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-21469-w)).
The core physics — a single published equation — is retained, while ICESHEET's recursive
flowline / crossover / saddle machinery is replaced by a grid eikonal solver (see
[Origin](#origin)).

It is meant as a *complementary first-guess tool* next to a full dynamic model (e.g.
[Yelmo](https://github.com/palma-ice/yelmo)) and an independent GIA / sea-level solver: to
seed a spin-up, force a surface-mass-balance model, or serve as a relaxation target.

## The idea

Under a perfectly-plastic, steady-state assumption, the **basal shear stress** $\tau_b$
balances the gravitational **driving stress** $\tau_d$ everywhere:

$$
\tau_b = \tau_d .
$$

The driving stress is fixed by the ice geometry — for surface elevation $z_s$, bed
elevation $z_b$, and thickness $H = z_s - z_b$, $\tau_d = \rho_i\, g\, H\, |\nabla z_s|$ —
so the prescribed $\tau_b$ relates to the surface as $\tau_b = \rho_i\, g\, H\, |\nabla z_s|$.
Rearranging for the surface slope gives the equation we solve:

$$
|\nabla z_s| = \frac{\tau_b}{\rho_i\, g\, H} = \frac{\tau_b}{\rho_i\, g\, (z_s - z_b)} .
$$

This is a static **Hamilton–Jacobi (eikonal) equation**. Solving it with an upwind grid
scheme means saddles, domes, and coalescing ice caps emerge automatically from the
viscosity solution — there is no flowline tracing, crossover detection, or saddle
bookkeeping. See [Algorithms](algorithms.qmd) for the full derivation and numerics.

An optional **DIVA closure** layered on the fixed geometry recovers what perfect plasticity
discards — a depth-averaged velocity and a first-order surface mass balance — and its dual
can drive the geometry *from* an SMB instead of a yield stress
([Algorithms §8–9](algorithms.qmd#sec-closures)).

## Quick start

```julia
using PlasticIceSheet

nx, ny = 100, 100
dx = dy = 2000.0                                   # grid spacing (m)
bed  = zeros(nx, ny)                               # bed elevation B (m)
mask = [hypot(i-50.5, j-50.5) <= 40 for i in 1:nx, j in 1:ny]
τ_b    = 1.0e5                                       # basal shear stress (Pa)

E, H = solve(bed, τ_b, mask; dx, dy, mode = :full)     # :full (default) or :flat
```

A full Hamilton–Jacobi solve on a 300×300 grid takes ~0.1 s. The basal shear stress
$\tau_b$ is differentiable, enabling gradient-based [inversion](usage.qmd#inverting-for).

## Origin

This is a clean-room simplification and modernization of
**[ICESHEET 2.0](https://github.com/evangowan/icesheet)**
([Gowan et al. 2016, *GMD*](https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-9-1673-2016);
[Gowan et al. 2021, *Nat. Commun.*](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-21469-w)). The core
physics — one published equation — is retained; the ~2,600-line recursive flowline /
crossover / saddle machinery is replaced by a grid eikonal solver on the order of a couple
hundred lines. The [Design notes](PLAN.md) record what was kept, simplified, and dropped,
and why.

`PlasticIceSheet.jl` is MIT-licensed.
